draft.introduction: Nizami Ganjavi’s Layla and Majnun (1188 CE) retells the pre-Islamic Arabic love story as a Persian masnavi. Majnun’s love for Layla drives him to madness and desert wandering; the Sufi tradition reads the tale as allegory for the soul’s consuming love for God.

translation.text: O God, illuminate my way! Let me not stumble in the darkness of myself. Grant me a tongue to speak of love, for love is the astrolabe of God’s mysteries. Whether love’s nature is human or divine, in the end it leads us to the Beloved’s door. Even the one who has lost his way in love has found a path to the truth.

translator_notes.note: The Sufi reading of Majnun’s madness (junun) transforms apparent insanity into the highest form of spiritual perception: the lover who has lost himself in the Beloved sees reality more clearly than the sane. This parallels the Pauline concept of “foolishness” (1 Corinthians 1:18–25) and the holy fool tradition in Eastern Christianity.
